title: Why We Demand Perfect Machines Yet Tolerate Human Carnage | NOEMA
gist: A neurosurgeon argues that autonomous vehicles like Waymo are already dramatically safer than human drivers by the data, but adoption is stalled by a 'feeling safe vs. being safe' perception gap that healthcare spent 30 years learning to manage, and prescribes standardized crash-rate reporting and radical transparency as the fix.

themes.theme: The perception gap: being safe vs. feeling safe

insights.claim: During a December power outage that killed traffic signals across a third of San Francisco, Waymo vehicles treated dark intersections as four-way stops and sometimes waited for remote-operator confirmation, causing visible clustering and stalling that went viral and prompted a city supervisor to call for a hearing, even though no Waymo crashed or caused injury across more than 7,000 dark intersections navigated that night.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: Illustrates how a single visible, camera-friendly automation hiccup can dominate public narrative and slow regulatory/consumer trust even absent any actual harm — a recurring tail-risk pattern for AV and other visible-AI deployments.
insights.locator: On a Saturday night, five days before Christmas

insights.claim: On the same day as the San Francisco Waymo blackout incident, roughly 115 Americans died in crashes involving human drivers, a routine daily toll that received no news coverage, illustrating the double standard in scrutiny applied to machine versus human error.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: Quantifies the asymmetric media/political scrutiny gap between AV incidents and the much larger, normalized toll of human driving — a key argument for why AV safety data alone won't move adoption.
insights.locator: On the same day, if it was an average day

insights.claim: The central thesis of the piece is that 'being safe' and 'feeling safe' are distinct problems requiring different solutions, and that the autonomous vehicle industry's core adoption barrier is that strong safety data has not translated into public trust.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: Reframes the AV investment/adoption bottleneck as a perception-management problem, not a technology or data problem — implying capital and effort should shift toward trust-building, not just safety-metric improvement.
insights.quote: The autonomous vehicle industry's biggest adoption problem is that strong safety data hasn't led to more public trust. Making a safer machine feel safe is a different discipline.
insights.locator: The autonomous vehicle industry's biggest

insights.claim: Illusory superiority — the near-universal belief among drivers that they are above-average — causes people to benchmark autonomous vehicle safety against an idealized self-image rather than actual human driving statistics.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific, well-documented cognitive bias as the psychological mechanism blocking AV adoption — useful for designing trust/communications interventions rather than more data dumps.
insights.locator: Nearly every driver believes they are above average

themes.theme: Waymo's safety data and its limits

insights.claim: Across more than 220 million miles, Waymo's vehicles have been involved in 94% fewer crashes causing serious injury or worse than human drivers on the same roads, with pedestrian injuries down 93%, cyclist crashes down 84% and intersection crashes down 96%.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: Hard, large-sample safety data (94-96% reductions across crash categories) supports a long-term bull case for Waymo-style driverless deployment displacing human driving at scale.
insights.locator: Across more than 220 million miles

insights.claim: If Waymo's safety results hold as deployment scales, the author predicts the population-level health effect could rival historical public-health interventions like seat belts or the decline in smoking.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: Frames AV rollout as a potential public-health-scale intervention, which strengthens the case for policy support and insurance-market repricing in favor of driverless fleets.
insights.locator: If the results hold as deployment scales

insights.claim: Waymo is the only major autonomous vehicle operator (versus Tesla's robotaxis in Austin and other Texas areas, and Amazon's Zoox in Las Vegas and San Francisco) that voluntarily publishes the granular mileage, crash and geography data needed to properly compare safety against human drivers.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: A data-transparency moat that currently favors Waymo in regulatory and public trust terms over Tesla and Zoox, relevant to competitive positioning in the robotaxi race.
insights.locator: Of course, not all autonomous vehicles are the same

insights.claim: Waymo has had real technical failures: a June fleet-wide software recall and highway-driving suspension after cars entered closed construction zones, an April incident of an empty Waymo driving into high water in San Antonio, and an earlier case of a Waymo driving into wet concrete it misread as a road.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: Even the safety leader has recurring edge-case software failures requiring fleet-wide recalls — a real operational and headline risk to price into any AV-operator thesis, not just a perception problem.
insights.locator: Technical malfunctions still happen

insights.claim: Driverless Waymos have been involved in crashes causing two deaths and one serious injury, and in all three documented cases the Waymo was not at fault — the incidents involved other drivers speeding, running red lights while fleeing police, or rear-ending the Waymo.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: Establishes that Waymo's fatal-incident record to date is attributable to third-party human error, a key fact for liability, insurance and regulatory-framing debates around driverless fleets.
insights.locator: Driverless Waymos have been involved in crashes

insights.claim: The author, despite having publicly argued in The New York Times that resistance to autonomous vehicles is an ethical failure, still felt his own Waymo was 'broken' when it paused behind a construction dumpster and needed remote-operator assistance to proceed, even though it was functioning exactly as designed.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: A first-person admission that even an informed AV advocate viscerally distrusts correct machine caution — evidence that the perception gap is not just an uninformed-public problem but a deep, near-universal cognitive bias.
insights.locator: Only a month earlier, I had argued

themes.theme: Healthcare's trust playbook applied to AVs

insights.claim: Harvard's Dr. Thomas Lee, who helped build the modern study of patient experience at Press Ganey, found that clinical quality and patient experience are correlated but distinct, and that patients with bad care experiences are less likely to have positive clinical outcomes, less likely to follow physical therapy regimens, and more likely to have complications and hospital readmissions — even after adjusting for diagnosis.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: A validated healthcare precedent showing perceived trust directly affects real outcomes (readmissions, complications) — implying AV trust deficits could translate into real behavioral costs (avoidance, unsafe manual overrides), not just PR noise.
insights.locator: Dr. Thomas Lee, a Harvard professor

insights.claim: Press Ganey's analysis of patient comments found the biggest trust-killers were rarely clinical errors but small visible signals like a used bandage on the floor or a bloodstained coat — a 'semiotic failure' where the signals a system sends diverge from what it actually delivers, requiring its own metrics and interventions distinct from clinical safety.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: Introduces 'semiotic failure' as a named, measurable category distinct from actual safety failure — a transferable framework for diagnosing why AV incidents like the SF blackout dominate perception disproportionate to actual risk.
insights.quote: They were reminders that there is no such thing as perfect safety, but who wants that reminder when they are sick?
insights.locator: When Lee's team ran natural-language processing

insights.claim: The author argues the December San Francisco blackout footage of stalled Waymos functioned as the AV industry's equivalent of the 'bandage on the floor' — a visible signal of dysfunction that diverged from the underlying reality that no one was harmed.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: Directly maps a healthcare-trust concept onto the specific AV incident, giving a concrete diagnostic lens for why the blackout event damaged trust disproportionately to its actual safety significance.
insights.locator: In the December blackout, cellphone cameras captured

insights.claim: The author explicitly states that the experience of feeling safe is not merely a communications or marketing problem but is itself part of the underlying safety problem, and that healthcare demonstrates this discipline can be studied and applied with its own metrics.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: A direct call to action: AV companies should build and fund a 'felt safety' measurement discipline analogous to patient-experience scoring, not just improve engineering or PR messaging.
insights.quote: The experience of feeling safe isn't a communications or marketing problem; it's part of the safety problem.
insights.locator: Safer technology that isn't used

themes.theme: The historical adoption arc of safety technology

insights.claim: Every major automotive safety technology follows the same arc — initial resistance and peak anxiety that eventually fades until the technology feels obviously lifesaving — as seen with seat belts, anti-lock brakes and airbags.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: A repeatable historical pattern investors and policymakers can use to time when AV trust is likely to normalize, based on comparable past technology-adoption curves.
insights.quote: Every major automotive safety technology follows the same arc. Initial resistance leads to peak anxiety that eventually fades until the tech itself feels obviously lifesaving.
insights.locator: Every major automotive safety technology follows

insights.claim: A 1974 US law requiring a seatbelt interlock device that blocked car starts unless front-seat occupants were belted worked as intended but felt intrusive and patronizing (e.g., triggered by grocery bags), and the backlash was so intense Congress outlawed the device within a year — a cautionary case that ignoring how a technology feels can set adoption back years even when it prevents harm.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: A specific historical warning against forcing safety technology on the public without managing the felt experience — directly applicable to AV mandates or aggressive remote-intervention design choices.
insights.locator: Trent Victor, Waymo's director of safety science

insights.claim: First-generation airbags deployed at consistently high force and did injure or kill some people, mostly children and small adults sitting close to the dashboard, before regulators allowed reduced deployment power and later adjustable, occupant-sensing designs reaching most cars by 1998 — showing early public fears about a new safety tech were directionally correct but wrong about magnitude.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: A precedent that genuine safety-tech harms can and should be engineered away over time rather than used as grounds for abandonment — an analog for how AV edge-case failures (e.g., construction-zone or flood incidents) should be handled.
insights.locator: With airbags, fears weren't entirely imagined

insights.claim: Exposure closes the fear gap: most Americans say they'd be uncomfortable with AI-assisted diagnosis, yet patients who actually receive AI-assisted screening report satisfaction above 90%, and confidence in self-driving cars runs far higher among people who have actually ridden in one than among non-riders.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: Direct experience, not information campaigns, is what converts skeptics — implies AV and AI-health adoption strategy should prioritize getting people into first rides/first uses over abstract safety messaging.
insights.locator: Eventually, exposure closes the fear gap

insights.claim: New York's Vision Zero redesigns starting in 2014 initially increased total crashes and minor injuries while reducing fatalities, and Oslo (starting 2015) and Helsinki (over decades) achieved entire years with zero pedestrian and cyclist deaths by 2019, with Helsinki recording an entire year with zero road deaths of any kind from July 2024 to July 2025 — its first since record-keeping began in 1960.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: Shows that street-design safety interventions can look worse on raw crash-count metrics short-term while dramatically cutting fatalities — a template for how AV safety metrics might also need reframing (severity-weighted, not raw incident counts).
insights.locator: New York City's Vision Zero program

insights.claim: Behavioral scientists call it 'algorithm aversion': people lose confidence in a machine faster than in a human after an identical mistake, in part because human errors have a responsible agent who can be blamed and eventually forgiven, while machine errors are experienced as opaque, unattributable failures with no one to forgive.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: Names a specific, literature-backed bias (algorithm aversion) that will make any single high-profile AV or AI-medical error disproportionately damaging versus an equivalent human error — a risk factor to monitor for reputational/regulatory shocks.
insights.locator: Empathy is a cognitive tool we use to forgive

insights.claim: An identical bad medical outcome will feel categorically different to patients and families when produced by an AI versus a trusted physician, even when the AI is measurably more accurate, because the AI currently lacks a track record and human presence to help interpret uncertainty.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: Predicts continued resistance to AI-driven clinical and autonomous decisions even as accuracy improves, until AI systems build an interpretable 'track record' analogous to individual physician reputation.
insights.locator: This suggests that an adverse outcome

themes.theme: Policy prescription: standardized reporting and radical transparency

insights.claim: Current federal rules require counting autonomous vehicle crashes but do not require operators to report the corresponding miles driven or where those miles occurred, leaving a 'numerator with no denominator' that prevents meaningful safety comparisons across AV operators.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific, fixable regulatory gap (mandatory per-mile, per-location crash-rate reporting) that would materially improve public and investor ability to compare AV operators' actual safety.
insights.locator: To address the gap between felt and actual safety

insights.claim: The author calls for the federal government to require every AV operator to report crash rates per mile driven and by location, verified against police and insurance records, following the precedent of Medicare's 2008 mandate that hospitals publicly report standardized patient-experience surveys, which by 2012 was tied to hospital payment levels.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: A concrete regulatory template (Medicare's patient-experience reporting-to-payment linkage) that could be replicated for AV operators, which investors should watch for as a future policy catalyst affecting Tesla, Zoox and Waymo alike.
insights.locator: Good reporting is only the floor

insights.claim: In October 2023, a Cruise (GM subsidiary) driverless car ran over a pedestrian who had been thrown into its path by a hit-and-run driver, then dragged her about 20 feet while pulling to the curb; Cruise's crash report to regulators omitted the dragging, leading the Justice Department to charge Cruise with providing a false record, a $500,000 fine, three years of federal oversight, suspended California permits, executive resignations including the CEO, and GM ultimately winding the company down.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: A cautionary case study: it was the cover-up, not the underlying accident, that destroyed Cruise's business — reinforcing that transparent incident disclosure is existential for AV operators' regulatory survival, not optional PR.
insights.locator: In October 2023, a driverless car operated

insights.claim: The University of Michigan health system's 2001 shift to proactively disclosing its own medical errors to patients and offering compensation, rather than fighting every malpractice claim, led to falling new claims and lawsuits and faster resolution times by 2010, contrary to fears that disclosure would enlarge liability.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: Empirical evidence that proactive error disclosure reduces rather than increases legal liability — a directly transferable playbook for AV operators fearing that transparency about incidents will increase lawsuit exposure.
insights.locator: In 2001, the University of Michigan's health system

insights.claim: Autonomous vehicle adoption will affect millions of Americans who drive for a living, with job losses expected to land earliest and hardest on older, lower-paid workers, even though economists frame this as 'creative destruction' (the subject of the 2025 Nobel Prize in Economics) that historically generates new job types and rising living standards over time.
insights.speaker: JONATHAN SLOTKIN
insights.so_what: Flags driving-job displacement as a genuine, non-perception-based political risk to AV rollout — distinct from the trust/perception problem, and one that safety data cannot resolve, implying need for explicit transition/workforce policy.
insights.locator: Being honest with the public also means
